label: Wiseman
name: Donald J. Wiseman
description: Professor of Assyriology at the University of London. Uniquely combined cuneiform expertise with evangelical biblical scholarship. TOTC 1 and 2 Kings.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: Donald John Wiseman (1918–2010) was Professor of Assyriology at the University of London and one of the foremost Assyriologists of the twentieth century. A committed evangelical Christian, he uniquely combined deep expertise in cuneiform texts and ancient Near Eastern archaeology with careful biblical scholarship. He served as chairman of the British School of Archaeology in Iraq and edited the journalIraqfor decades.

bio_sections.title: Interpretive Approach
bio_sections.body: Wiseman reads 1–2 Kings with unmatched command of the Assyrian, Babylonian, and Egyptian source material that illuminates the biblical narrative. His TOTC commentary integrates archaeological discoveries — seal impressions, annals, king lists, treaty forms — to show how the biblical text fits within the broader ancient Near Eastern world. He treats the text as historically reliable while acknowledging interpretive difficulties.

bio_sections.title: Theological Tradition
bio_sections.body: Wiseman writes from a conservative evangelical perspective, affirming the historical reliability of the biblical narrative while engaging seriously with ancient Near Eastern evidence. His approach is characterized by meticulous philological care and a conviction that archaeology consistently supports rather than undermines the biblical record.

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: 1 and 2 Kings(Tyndale OT Commentary, 1993) — Concise, archaeologically informed commentary. Also:Chronicles of Chaldaean Kings(1956),Nebuchadrezzar and Babylon(1985).

bio_sections.title: Appears In
bio_sections.body: Wiseman notes appear throughout 1 Kings and 2 Kings.

label: Provan
name: Iain W. Provan
description: Marshall Sheppard Professor at Regent College. Literary-theological reading of Kings with attention to Deuteronomistic narrative and canonical context.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: Iain William Provan is Marshall Sheppard Professor of Biblical Studies at Regent College, Vancouver. A Scottish-born Old Testament scholar, he previously held chairs at the University of Edinburgh. Provan is known for combining rigorous historical-critical scholarship with a deep commitment to reading Scripture as canonical literature that addresses the contemporary church.

bio_sections.title: Interpretive Approach
bio_sections.body: Provan reads 1–2 Kings as a theological narrative rather than merely a historical chronicle. His NIBCOT commentary focuses on the text’s literary artistry — narrative pacing, characterization, irony, and structural design — while always asking what the Deuteronomistic Historian intended the reader to learn theologically. He is particularly strong on the theological evaluation of kings and the prophetic word as the engine that drives the narrative.

bio_sections.title: Theological Tradition
bio_sections.body: Provan writes from a Reformed evangelical perspective with particular interest in the philosophy of history. He has argued vigorously (inA Biblical History of Israel, co-authored with Long and Longman) that the biblical narratives should be treated as credible historical testimony rather than dismissed as late theological construction.

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: 1 and 2 Kings(New International Biblical Commentary OT, 1995) — Literary-theological commentary with attention to narrative structure and Deuteronomistic themes. Also:A Biblical History of Israel(2003, with Long and Longman).

bio_sections.title: Appears In
bio_sections.body: Provan notes appear throughout 1 Kings and 2 Kings.

label: Japhet
name: Sara Japhet
tradition: Critical · OTL I–II Chronicles · Hebrew University
description: Professor of Bible at Hebrew University of Jerusalem (1934–2022). The definitive critical commentary on Chronicles. Demonstrated the Chronicler’s literary artistry and theological independence.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: Sara Japhet (1934–2022) was Professor of Bible at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem and one of the most distinguished biblical scholars of the 20th century. Born in Czechoslovakia and educated in Israel, she became the world's foremost authority on the books of Chronicles, devoting her career to demonstrating the Chronicler's literary artistry and theological independence.

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: Her magisterial commentary on I & II Chronicles in the Old Testament Library (OTL) series (1993) is considered the definitive critical treatment. Her earlier monographThe Ideology of the Book of Chronicles and Its Place in Biblical Thought(1977/1989) established the framework that all subsequent Chronicles scholarship has engaged. She argued convincingly that the Chronicler was not merely copying Samuel-Kings but creating an independent theological work with its own sources and purposes.

bio_sections.title: Approach
bio_sections.body: Japhet is meticulous on textual detail, source analysis, and the Chronicler's editorial technique. She demonstrates how the Chronicler reshaped inherited traditions to address the concerns of the post-exilic community: identity, legitimate worship, and the continuity of God's promises. Her commentary is essential for understanding the differences between Chronicles and Samuel-Kings — not as errors but as deliberate theological choices.

label: Selman
name: Martin J. Selman
description: Director of Postgraduate Studies at Spurgeon’s College, London. TOTC 1 and 2 Chronicles: the Chronicler’s theological agenda made accessible.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: Martin J. Selman served as Director of Postgraduate Studies at Spurgeon's College, London, and as a lecturer in Old Testament at the college for many years. His academic focus on the historical books of the Old Testament, particularly Chronicles, made him one of the leading evangelical voices on the Chronicler's theology and purpose.

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: His two-volume commentary on 1 & 2 Chronicles in the Tyndale Old Testament Commentary (TOTC) series (1994) is widely regarded as one of the best mid-level evangelical treatments of Chronicles. Selman excels at making the Chronicler's theological agenda accessible — explaining why the genealogies matter, why David is presented differently than in Samuel, and how the post-exilic community found identity in these texts.

bio_sections.title: Approach
bio_sections.body: Selman reads Chronicles as purposeful theological history rather than a defective retelling of Samuel-Kings. He emphasises the Chronicler's distinctive themes: "all Israel" as a united worshipping community, David as liturgist rather than warrior, the centrality of the temple, and the Levites as guardians of Israel's spiritual heritage. His commentary balances careful historical research with pastoral application.

label: Clines
name: David J.A. Clines
description: WBC Job (3 vols). The most comprehensive modern commentary on any biblical book.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: David Clines is Emeritus Professor of Biblical Studies at the University of Sheffield, one of the most prolific Old Testament scholars of the late 20th century. He is also editor of theDictionary of Classical Hebrew(8 volumes).

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: His three-volume WBC Job (1989, 2006, 2011) totals over 2,600 pages — the most comprehensive modern commentary on any biblical book.

bio_sections.title: Approach
bio_sections.body: Clines reads Job as sophisticated literature that resists systematic theology. He argues that God never actually answers Job’s question, that the epilogue is deliberately ironic, and that the friends are not simply wrong. His approach is philologically rigorous and theologically provocative.

label: Habel
name: Norman C. Habel
description: OTL Job. Reads Job as a legal drama — cosmic court case ending in theophany.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: Norman Habel is an Australian Lutheran theologian and biblical scholar known for pioneering eco-theology and the Earth Bible project.

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: His Old Testament Library commentary on Job (1985) remains a standard academic reference.

bio_sections.title: Approach
bio_sections.body: Habel’s key insight is that Job is structured as a legal drama. Job functions as plaintiff, God as defendant, the friends as expert witnesses. The legal vocabulary throughout — witness, arbiter, redeemer, oath, verdict — supports this reading.

label: Longman
name: Tremper Longman III
description: NICOT Ecclesiastes and Song of Solomon. Reformed evangelical with literary sensitivity.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: Tremper Longman III is Distinguished Scholar and Professor Emeritus of Biblical Studies at Westmont College. He has authored or co-authored over thirty books, including commentaries, introductions, and theological studies across the Old Testament.

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: His NICOT Ecclesiastes (1998) and NICOT Song of Solomon (2001) are standard evangelical commentaries. He also co-authoredAn Introduction to the Old Testamentwith Raymond Dillard, one of the most widely used OT survey texts.

bio_sections.title: Approach
bio_sections.body: Longman reads Qoheleth as a character within the book whose pessimistic observations are framed and corrected by the epilogue narrator (12:9–14). The “fear God and keep his commandments” conclusion recontextualises everything Qoheleth said. His approach is literary-canonical: the final form of the text, not just Qoheleth’s voice, carries the theological message.

label: Fox
name: Michael V. Fox
description: JPS Ecclesiastes. The definitive philological commentary on Qoheleth.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: Michael V. Fox is the Jay C. and Ruth Halls Bascom Professor Emeritus of Hebrew at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. He is one of the leading Hebraists of his generation, with expertise spanning wisdom literature, Egyptian literature, and Semitic philology.

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: HisA Time to Tear Down and a Time to Build Up(1999) and the JPS commentary on Qohelet (2004) together constitute the most comprehensive philological analysis of Ecclesiastes in English.

bio_sections.title: Approach
bio_sections.body: Fox’s signature contribution is the argument thathebelmeans “absurd” rather than “meaningless” or “vain.” Life is not empty but irrational — it defies the human expectation that effort should produce proportionate results. This reading transforms Ecclesiastes from nihilism into existential protest, closer in spirit to Camus than to despair.

label: Garrett
name: Duane A. Garrett
description: NAC Song of Songs. Reads it as a unified love poem celebrating marital intimacy.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: Duane Garrett is John R. Sampey Professor of Old Testament Interpretation at The Southern Baptist Theological Seminary in Louisville, Kentucky. He has authored commentaries on Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and Song of Songs, as well as studies in Hosea and Joel.

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: His NAC Song of Songs (2004) reads the poem as a unified dramatic sequence celebrating marital love. He also contributed the NAC Proverbs volume and has written on OT introduction and Hebrew exegesis.

bio_sections.title: Approach
bio_sections.body: Garrett reads Song of Songs as a single poem with a dramatic arc, not a loose anthology. He identifies the speakers (Beloved, Lover, Daughters of Jerusalem) and traces their relationship through courtship, wedding, and consummation. His approach is literary-evangelical: the text is both great love poetry and a theological statement about the goodness of marital intimacy as God designed it.

label: VanGemeren
name: Willem A. VanGemeren
description: EBC Psalms. Comprehensive evangelical commentary integrating ANE background with Reformed theology.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: Willem VanGemeren is Professor Emeritus of Old Testament and Semitic Languages at Trinity Evangelical Divinity School. He edited the five-volumeNew International Dictionary of Old Testament Theology and Exegesis(NIDOTTE), one of the standard reference works for OT studies.

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: His EBC Psalms (2008) is a comprehensive evangelical commentary that integrates ancient Near Eastern background, Hebrew philology, and Reformed theological reflection.

bio_sections.title: Approach
bio_sections.body: VanGemeren reads the Psalms as both historical worship documents and prophetic texts pointing to Christ. He traces royal, messianic, and eschatological themes through the Psalter while attending to each psalm’s literary structure and emotional texture.

label: Goldingay
name: John Goldingay
description: BCOT Psalms (3 vols). Literary-theological commentary with fresh translation.

bio_sections.title: Biography
bio_sections.body: John Goldingay is David Allan Hubbard Professor Emeritus of Old Testament at Fuller Theological Seminary. Born in Birmingham, England, he taught at St John’s College, Nottingham before moving to Fuller in 1997.

bio_sections.title: Key Works
bio_sections.body: His three-volume Baker Commentary on Psalms (2006\u20132008) offers a fresh translation of every psalm with literary and theological commentary. He has also authored major works on Daniel, Isaiah 40\u201355, and Old Testament theology.

bio_sections.title: Approach
bio_sections.body: Goldingay’s approach is literary-theological: he attends to each psalm’s poetic structure, metaphorical language, and emotional register before drawing theological conclusions. His fresh translations often reveal nuances hidden by familiar renderings.

label: Collins
name: John J. Collins
description: Leading Daniel scholar; Hermeneia commentary. Expert on Jewish apocalypticism.

description: Lamentations as trauma literature; pioneering grief-theology scholar.

label: Oswalt

description: Leading evangelical Isaiah scholar; NICOT commentary (2 vols). Defends unified authorship.

label: Childs

description: Pioneer of canonical criticism; reads Isaiah as unified theological whole regardless of authorship.
